data.title: PCEF: Understanding The Structure And Suitability Of This CEF ETF
data.description:
The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF (PCEF) is an exchange-traded index fund that aims to track the price and yield performance of the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. As the name of the index suggests, this is an index that consists of closed-end funds that manage assets on behalf of investors in an attempt to obtain money that is used to pay a distribution to investors. As might be expected based on this description, the index will generally have one of the highest yields of any asset available in the American markets. The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF invests its assets into the securities that comprise this index, so naturally it receives the distributions that they pay out. PCEF then distributes this money to its own investors, after deducting its expenses from the total. As is the case with most Invesco funds, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF has a very reasonable expense ratio, so this results in PCEF itself having one of the highest yields that can be obtained from an exchange-traded fund in the American markets. This could make the fund a reasonably attractive investment for an investor who is seeking to earn a high level of income from the assets in their portfolio.

About The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF

As just stated in the introduction, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is an exchange-traded index fund that aims to track the price and yield performance of the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. The fund’s website offers the following description:

The Invesco CEF Composite ETF is based on the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. The Fund will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in securities of funds included in the Index. The Fund is a “fund-of-funds,” as it invests its assets in the common shares of funds included in the Index rather than in individual securities. The Index currently includes closed-end funds that invest in taxable investment grade fixed-income securities, taxable high yield fixed-income securities and others that utilize an equity option writing (selling) strategy. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.

As stated in the description, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is a fund of funds. This is a designation that has long been used in the hedge fund world, but it is much less common to see such a fund trading in the public markets. Investopedia offers a description of a fund of funds:

A fund of funds is one that, instead of investing in a pool of securities like stocks and bonds, buys shares in other funds. These “multi-manager” investments offer investors further diversification and access to the expertise of other skilled fund managers.

In short, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF does not invest in common equities, preferred stocks, or bonds like most other exchange-traded funds in the market. Rather, it invests in the common equity shares of closed-end funds that own these assets. As Investopedia points out, this could offer investors a diversification advantage. Rather than purchasing a single closed-end fund and relying on the performance of that fund’s individual manager as a source of investment returns, PCEF provides investors with a way to put their money into many different funds at the same time. The advantage here is that the performance of PCEF should be less affected by the performance of any individual fund manager, and strong performance from a manager who performs well could offset weak performance from a manager who performs poorly during a given period of time. As there is no way to accurately predict ahead of time which managers will do well and which will not during a given period, this proposition may be favorable to an investor who wishes to reduce specific risk (as opposed to systematic risk).

However, there is a downside to the fund of funds structure. This is that the expenses incurred by investors tend to be fairly high. This comes from the fact that each layer of funds has its own expenses. For example, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF has a total expense ratio of 2.76%, but not all of this is due to the fees that Invesco imposes on the fund. In fact, PCEF itself only has a management fee of 0.50%. The reason for the 2.76% total expense ratio is that the closed-end funds in which PCEF invests have their own expenses, and as they are typically actively managed funds that employ leverage, these funds have much higher expense ratios than most exchange-traded index funds. For example, these are the largest positions in the portfolio of the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF as of March 17, 2026:

Here are the reported expense ratios for each of these funds, as reported in the funds’ respective fact sheets or websites as of March 18, 2026:

Fund Name

Reported Gross Expense Ratio

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (EXG)

1.07%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC)

3.39%

Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund (BXMX)

0.97%

BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust (BSTZ)

1.48%

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETV)

1.08%

BlackRock Capital Allocation Term Trust (BCAT)

1.47%

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund (ETY)

1.06%

BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Trust (BDJ)

0.88%

Nuveen NASDAQ 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (QQQX)

0.89%

PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY)

1.27%

For comparison purposes, the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), which is one of the largest exchange-traded index funds in the American markets, has a gross expense ratio of 0.0945%. Obviously, that is far below the expense ratios of any of the funds that comprise the ten largest positions in the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF. As PCEF owns the common equity of the funds in its portfolio, investors in PCEF indirectly incur both the expenses of PCEF itself and the funds that it includes in its portfolio. We can therefore see that the fund of funds strategy provides investors with diversification across fund closed-end fund managers, but it also results in these same investors indirectly incurring fairly high fees relative to other options. The website for the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF states that the fund’s pro rata share of the expenses incurred by the underlying funds in its portfolio is 2.26%. When combined with PCEF’s own 0.50% management fee, we arrive at a total expense ratio of 2.76% for the fund itself. PCEF currently has a fee waiver and expense reimbursement in place with its adviser until August 31, 2027, which reduces its expense ratio by 0.05%. Thus, as of March 18, 2026, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF has a net expense ratio of 2.71%, which is higher than what many investors in exchange-traded funds expect.

The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is not an especially large exchange-traded fund, as it only has approximately $784.32 million in assets under management. This is somewhat larger than the Saba Closed-End Funds ETF (CEFS), which is one of the few other exchange-traded funds that invests in closed-end funds. Here are a few other exchange-traded funds that also invest in this asset class and how they compare in terms of size:

Fund Name

Assets Under Management

Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF

$784.32 million

Saba Closed-End Funds ETF

$376.60 million

VanEck CEF Muni Income ETF (XMPT)

$218.02 million

VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD)

$1.42 billion

(figures are as of March 17, 2026)

As clearly shown, the only one of these exchange-traded funds that is larger than the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is the VanEck BDC Income ETF. However, it is questionable whether or not that fund is completely comparable to PCEF. As the name of that fund suggests, the VanEck BDC Income ETF invests its assets in business development companies, rather than the closed-end funds that comprise PCEF’s portfolio. While this is true, we should keep in mind that business development companies are actually just a specialized form of closed-end fund. For example:

  1. Business Development Companies and Closed-End Funds are both regulated under the U.S. Investment Company Act of 1940.
  2. Business Development Companies and Closed-End Funds are both closed-ended companies that make their money by actively investing in assets rather than producing a product or service.
  3. Business Development Companies and Closed-End Funds can both opt to be treated as regulated investment companies for tax purposes.

The difference between closed-end funds mostly has to do with the types of assets in which they invest. A closed-end fund usually invests in publicly traded assets such as common equities, preferred equities, and bonds. A business development company, on the other hand, typically invests in both debt and equity issued by small or mid-sized companies, including privately held companies. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission states on its website that business development companies are also legally permitted to use higher levels of debt and leverage than most closed-end funds. In addition to this, business development companies frequently file quarterly financial reports, whereas a typical closed-end fund files such reports semiannually. As such, investors in business development companies may have access to more recent financial information to assist them in making their investment decisions.

The VanEck CEF Muni Income ETF also has some notable differences when compared to PCEF. The most important among these is that XMPT tracks the price and yield performance of the S-Network Municipal Bond Closed-End Fund Index. This is a different index than PCEF tracks, and this particular index only includes those closed-end funds that invest in federal income tax-exempt municipal bonds. The Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF, on the other hand, invests in both investment-grade bond funds and junk bond funds as well as closed-end equity funds that write covered call options against its positions. Thus, PCEF provides exposure to a much wider range of potential assets and funds. This is also evident in the fact that PCEF had 111 holdings on March 16, 2026, compared to 40 for XMPT. However, PCEF will not provide the same level of tax benefits as XMPT, so investors for whom taxation is a major concern may wish to keep that in mind. Anyone who is planning to hold their shares of PCEF in a retirement account will not need to be concerned about this.

The Saba Closed-End Funds ETF is an actively-managed fund that employs an activist strategy to earn profits for investors. That particular exchange-traded fund typically purchases shares of closed-end funds that are trading at a discount to their net asset value. Saba Capital is then known to push on the management of some of the closed-end funds that it holds across its various investment vehicles to make changes that are intended to close the discounts that those funds trade at relative to their net asset values. This is a very different strategy from the passive indexing strategy used by PCEF.

In addition to their size and investment strategies, the four peer exchange-traded funds shown above have very different yields. This chart shows the yields paid by each of these funds as of March 18, 2026:

Fund Name

Yield As Of March 18, 2026

Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF

8.08%

Saba Closed-End Funds ETF

7.97%

VanEck CEF Muni Income ETF

5.77%

VanEck BDC Income ETF

12.90%

We can see that the exchange-traded fund that primarily focuses on business development companies has a significantly higher yield than any of the other funds. The fund that only invests in closed-end funds that focus on municipal bonds is the lowest-yielding of the group. This is somewhat unsurprising, as municipal bonds in general tend to have lower yields than most other bonds due to the fact that investors do not need to pay income taxes on the coupons that they receive. This makes municipal bonds rather desirable assets among high-income investors. The other two funds had relatively similar yields as of March 18, 2026.

Many investors generally assume that there is a correlation between an exchange-traded fund’s assets under management and the fund’s liquidity. For example, fund management company AllianceBernstein makes the following statement in an article published on its website:

When an exchange-traded fund is first launched, its lower trading volume and smaller amount of assets under management are often a concern for investors looking to invest sizable amounts.

If we look solely at the average daily volume, we certainly see a correlation between a fund’s assets under management and its average daily volume. This is clearly visible if we look at the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF and its peers:

Fund Name

Average Daily Volume (# of Shares)

Average Daily Volume (USD)

Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF

132,756

$2,564,846

Saba Closed-End Funds ETF

101,233

$2,288,878

VanEck CEF Muni Income ETF

57,159

$1,260,356

VanEck BDC Income ETF

3.41 million

$43,989,000

(U.S. dollar figures are calculated using the March 18, 2026, closing price for each of the funds)

As we can clearly see, the largest of the funds (BIZD) has a substantially higher average daily volume than any of the other funds. The second-largest of the funds, PCEF, has the second-highest average daily volume, although it is substantially lower than that of BIZD. However, at approximately $2.56 million worth of shares traded per day, the Invesco CEF Income composite ETF should have sufficient liquidity to satisfy the needs of most retail investors. After all, the average retail investor is not purchasing several hundred thousand dollars worth of an exchange-traded fund in a single trade.

In fact, it is unlikely that the average retail investor needs to worry about the liquidity of most exchange-traded funds. As AllianceBernstein points out in the article that I linked right before the table, all exchange-traded funds have contracted with a market maker that is known as an authorized participant. The authorized participant has the ability to purchase shares of the ETF in the market and exchange them for the fund’s underlying assets. It does this whenever the fund’s share price is below its net asset value, and the expectation is that the buying pressure on the fund’s shares will lift the price. The authorized participant also has the ability to do this trade in reverse. It can purchase the underlying assets that make up the fund (known as creation units) and then deliver them to the fund in exchange for shares (the fund creates these shares whenever the assets are delivered). The authorized participant then sells the shares that it received in the market. The selling pressure reduces the ETF’s share price. In effect, the authorized participant is engaging in arbitrage, as it makes money no matter which action it takes, and it assumes virtually no risk to earn this money. Due to the presence of the authorized participant, exchange-traded fund investors do not usually need to worry about the liquidity of a given fund. The only real situation in which liquidity might become an issue is if the underlying assets of the exchange-traded fund are not especially liquid. Closed-end funds, in general, are admittedly not the most liquid assets trading in the public markets, but they should still be liquid enough that retail investors in PCEF should not need to worry about whether or not their individual trade will move the fund’s share price away from the actual value of the fund’s underlying assets. It is, however, possible that a large enough trade could move the price of the underlying assets.

Understanding The S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index

As stated earlier, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is an exchange-traded index fund that aims to track the price and yield performance of the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. As such, we should probably take a few moments to look at this index in order to gain a better understanding of how PCEF may perform in real-world conditions.

The website for the index offers the following description:

The S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index is a fund index designed to serve as a benchmark for closed-end funds listed in the US that principally engage in asset management processes seeking to produce taxable annual yield.

This description clearly states that the index is targeting closed-end funds that aim to provide a high level of current income for their investors. As such, we would expect that the closed-end funds that are included in this index will have relatively high yields, at least when compared to many of the other assets that are available in the American capital markets. This is indeed the case, which we can see by looking at the yields of the top ten constituents of this index. Here they are:

As of March 17, 2026, these closed-end funds were the largest constituents of the index. We can see that most of these names are pretty similar to what PCEF has among its largest holdings. This is expected to be the case due to the simple fact that PCEF is an exchange-traded index fund, and index funds usually achieve their objectives by simply purchasing all of the constituents of an index in relatively equal proportion to what the index possesses. However, we can see that the index has a few slight differences from the fund’s holdings. In particular, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund was not included among the index’s largest constituents as of March 17, 2026, but it was included among PCEF’s top ten largest positions as of the same date one year earlier. Meanwhile, the BlackRock Science & Technology Trust (BST), which was one of the largest constituents in the index on March 17, 2026, was not included among PCEF’s largest positions as of the same date in 2025. For the most part, though, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF has a portfolio that is pretty similar to that of the benchmark index.

Here are the yields of the funds that comprise the ten largest constituents of the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index as of March 18, 2026:

Fund Name

Yield As Of March 18, 2026

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund

8.77%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

9.32%

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund

8.37%

BlackRock Science and Technology Term Trust

8.90%

Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund

7.76%

Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Diversified Equity Income Fund

8.35%

BlackRock Capital Allocation Term Trust

20.92%

BlackRock Enhanced Equity Dividend Trust

8.33%

Nuveen NASDAQ 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund

8.94%

BlackRock Science & Technology Trust

7.72%

The S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index is an index of closed-end funds that primarily focus on providing their investors with a very high level of current income. As we can see from the yields among these funds, it does appear that it is doing exactly that.

We should point out, however, that the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index does not claim to include all closed-end funds in the American equity market, nor does it attempt to do so. The index includes closed-end funds in four specific categories:

  1. Investment-grade bond funds,
  2. Junk bond funds,
  3. Preferred stock funds,
  4. Equity funds that employ a covered call-writing strategy.

There are several types of closed-end funds that are strictly excluded from this list. For example, the index does not include commodity funds such as the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS). It does not include common equity funds such as the Adams Diversified Equity Fund (ADX), and it does not include convertible bond funds such as the Advent Convertible and Income Fund (AVK). Thus, the index excludes a number of funds that may be popular among certain investors. However, equity closed-end funds (and convertible closed-end funds) are typically focused on providing investors with capital gains rather than income. While some equity and some convertible bond funds can offer yields that are comparable to the funds shown above, they finance their distributions by realizing capital gains on their assets rather than receiving direct payments in the form of bond coupons or option premiums.

We should also keep in mind that the index is exclusively comprised of closed-end funds that trade natively on a major American exchange (such as the New York Stock Exchange) and are regulated under the Investment Company Act of 1940. There are some foreign companies out there such as RIT Capital Partners (RITPF) or Pershing Square Holdings (PSHZF) that have similar business models to American closed-end funds but do not natively trade on an American exchange. These companies are not eligible for inclusion in the index and thus they are not included in the portfolio of the Invesco CEF Composite ETF.

The methodology document for the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index explains in detail how the fund chooses the specific funds that will comprise the index and how it weights them. In particular, each fund’s assets must consist primarily of the types of securities previously mentioned and meet the following criteria:

  • The fund must have at least $75 million in assets under management,
  • The fund’s share price must be within 25% of its net asset value for ten days prior to the rebalancing date,
  • The fund’s management fee must be below 1.50%,
  • The fund needs to have an average daily volume (measured in U.S. dollars) of at least $500,000,
  • The fund must have been trading in the market for three months,
  • Term funds must not have a termination date within the next three years.

These rules appear to be designed so that the index will exclude any very tightly held funds or funds for which liquidity may be an issue. As was already mentioned, exchange-traded funds can sometimes run into liquidity problems if the underlying assets are not especially liquid, so indices that are designed to be tracked by an exchange-traded fund usually have rules in place to ensure that the fund’s assets are mostly liquid ones.

The S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index employs a weighted net asset value system to determine the proportional weight of each fund that is included in the index. Thus, for the most part, the higher a fund’s net assets, the higher its weight in the index. However, the weight of any individual closed-end fund is capped at 8%. If any individual fund has a sufficiently large net asset value to otherwise be 8% of the index, then anything over 8% is proportionally distributed among the other funds that comprise the index. In addition to this rule, the index also has a rule that states that the total weight of all funds that would otherwise represent more than 5% of the index shall be capped at 45% of the total weighting. This rule prevents a handful of funds from accounting for more than half of the index. It also works pretty well with the U.S. government’s regulation that forbids a regulated investment company from having its assets disproportionately allocated to only a handful of securities. Under Federal regulations, the total weighting of all securities accounting for more than 5% of an RIC’s portfolio cannot be above 50%. Due to this rule, many indices that are designed to be tracked by an index fund limit the weightings of the most heavily weighted securities in the portfolio to comply with this regulation. The S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index is included among this group. As was the case with the 8% rule, if the index has a few sufficiently large positions to be in violation of this rule, then any amount in excess of 45% is distributed among the smaller funds in the index. Finally, those closed-end funds that typically trade with a large discount to net asset value receive a slight increase in their weighting in the index and those closed-end funds that typically trade at a large premium to their net asset values receive a slight decrease in their weightings in the index.

The S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index is rebalanced and reconstituted on the last business day of each calendar quarter (in the months of March, June, September, and December). Presumably, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF adjusts its own portfolio on the first available opportunity after the index rebalancing.

Understanding Closed-End Funds

There are a few different types of investment funds in the market, and closed-end funds are the least common. The portfolio of the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF only invests in closed-end funds, though, so investors in the ETF should understand how they work.

A closed-end fund is somewhat similar to an exchange-traded fund in that both types of funds trade on a stock exchange, such as the New York Stock Exchange. The difference, however, comes from the fact that a closed-end fund has a fixed number of shares outstanding.

Earlier in this article, I explained how the authorized participant works to keep the share price of an exchange-traded fund in line with the value of its underlying assets. The arbitrage that the authorized participant engages in causes the number of shares outstanding of the fund to increase or decrease with investor demand and the fund’s net asset value. This is not the case for a closed-end fund. Rather, a closed-end fund issues a specific number of shares in a single issuance (this could be at the time that the fund is first established or through a regular share offering). The fund manager of the closed-end fund then invests the fund’s assets in accordance with the fund’s prospectus and objectives. The share price of a closed-end fund is determined by other market participants. As is technically the case with the common equity of a corporation, the share price of a closed-end fund simply depends on what another market participant will pay for it at any given time. This can, on occasion, result in a given fund’s share price being quite different from the actual value of its underlying assets. The share price of a closed-end fund thus does not necessarily go up whenever the fund’s portfolio gets larger due to capital gains. The fund’s share price also does not necessarily go down whenever the fund’s portfolio suffers losses.

Closed-end funds also pay out most or all of their net investment income and net realized capital gains to their investors. This usually results in these securities having some of the highest yields that are available in the market. While an exchange-traded fund technically has to do the same thing (as ETFs are usually regulated investment companies just like closed-end funds), many exchange-traded index funds rarely realize capital gains and so do not have any realized gains to pay out. Closed-end funds, on the other hand, are almost always actively managed funds that frequently realize their gains.

Tax Considerations

As already mentioned, the closed-end funds in the portfolio of the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF consist primarily of funds that invest primarily in fixed-income securities and covered call-writing funds. This could make PCEF somewhat problematic for investors who are in a high federal income tax bracket and are investing using a taxable account.

One of the characteristics of a regulated investment company is that they act as a pass-through entity for tax purposes. This means that the company itself does not pay corporate taxes. However, its distributions are then taxed as if the fund’s investors held the assets directly. This can be problematic for bond funds because coupon payments made by a bond are taxed at the ordinary income tax rate, rather than at favorable capital gains rates. The distributions that PCEF receives from the closed-end bond funds in its portfolio may therefore be considered ordinary income, depending on whether the money came from bond coupons, capital gains, or return of capital. When it distributes this money out to its own investors, those investors will therefore have to pay ordinary income taxes on these distributions. Ultimately, this means that investors in PCEF may have higher tax liability than they would if the fund paid out the same distribution yield but the money came from long-term realized capital gains.

Financial advisors typically recommend holding any funds that derive a significant proportion of their income from bonds in a retirement account or some other form of tax-advantaged account. While this fund’s income is not entirely from bonds (and thus the tax liability should be lower than it would be for a bond fund with the same yield), investors in high tax brackets might still want to consider using a retirement account to purchase the shares rather than an ordinary brokerage account.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Invesco CEF Income Composite ETF is an exchange-traded index fund that tracks the price and yield performance of the S-Network Composite Closed-End Fund Index. This is an index that is comprised of income-focused closed-end funds investing in bonds, preferred stocks, and covered call-writing strategies. The index specifically excludes equity and convertible bond funds, and it also excludes funds that do not have American listings. The fund will generally have a higher yield than many other ETFs, but some of its distributions will be taxed as ordinary income, and thus it might be advisable to hold its shares in a tax-advantaged account.

This article answers these three main questions about PCEF:

  • Why is the expense ratio for PCEF so high?
  • Is PCEF better suited for a regular investment account or a tax-advantaged account?
  • How are the holdings of PCEF chosen?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Critical Metals: Focus On Execution Reality Instead Of Speculative Story (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

It has been about 3 months since I first covered this company, and it has gained by over 24% since my past bullish forecast. However, in my previous article, I mentioned that one of the major risks to my cautiously optimistic outlook is the production timeline, which would determine if investors should stay hopeful or be concerned.

As of March 2026, the speculative "Buy" thesis for Critical Metals (CRML) is now at a pivotal point. While the stock has surged more than 150% year-to-date after high-grade re-assay results, the investment story for the Company should now shift from "anticipation" to seeking "verification," based on the production timeline, which is now facing a significant postponement.

Initial ore production, which was previously targeted for 2026, has been delayed to Q4 2028 or even Q1 2029, with concentrate exports not expected until Q3 2029. Despite the board’s approval of a $30 million strategic acceleration program, the three-year gap until revenue generation leaves the company prone to financial risks. Therefore, I am downgrading CRML to a "Hold" rating until the "Execution Gap" is closed by tangible operational milestones.

It is not a Sell because I would be keeping the Company in view, given some more positive developments such as the recent joint venture with the Romanian JV and the Tanbreez pilot plant, whose phase 1 is projected to be completed not later than May 2026. Now that nothing much will be happening in Phase 1.

Aspirational targets are far in the future

First of all, I still see value in Tanbreez mining preparation from many angles that lean towards optimism. Despite the postponement of Tanbreez production, $30 million has been allocated to advance this mining field towards production. Moreover, another aspect to note is the 50/50 joint venture CRML has entered into with Tariq Abdel Hadi Abdullah Al-Qahtani & Brothers Company (TQB) to establish a $1.5 billion REE processing factory. This joint venture also includes pre-selling 25% of Tanbreez’s future output as a long-time buyer. This now becomes a guaranteed customer and co-investor scenario, indicating that the ecosystem around production is gradually taking shape.

Also, Tanbreez remains one of the premier mining projects globally, with a total resource quantity of 28.2 million tonnes of oxides. This project maintains strong fundamentals, including a $3 billion net present value before tax and an internal rate of return (IRR) of 180% with a payback period of less than 3 years. Approval for exploitation has been given for 30 years, giving the company a long-term operational runway once the initial hurdles are cleared—the challenge remains the "execution gap."

Mining intelligence and the BMW agreement

In addition to rare earths, the lithium offtake agreement with BMW provides a near-term alleviation to CRML's revenue. BMW’s advanced payments of $15 million from this agreement show the strategic importance of the partnership, which grants BMW the right to purchase 100% of the lithium hydroxide produced. This revenue is still expected to begin in 2026 and may run for six years, but it is not yet material enough to offset the broader delays in the Tanbreez rare earth operations.

Cash runway challenge

The reason I am cautious is that the Company remains a pre-revenue one facing an extended timeline, even though the projects and joint venture mentioned may have potential in the future. CRML’s free cash flow has substantially deteriorated as of 31st December 2025 to -32.3 million from -$15.54 million in 2024. This is a testament that CRML is bleeding cash at the moment, with no income until 2028, when production is expected.

While the BMW contract may offer some recovery this year, the burn rate remains high. Currently, revenue primarily comes from other income rather than ore sales. With cash and equivalents of $80.9 million and quarterly cash burn from operation and investment of around $12 million. This leaves a cash runway of roughly 6 quarters—which might be a stretch given the gap to production-readiness.

Valuation

In my previous article, I made approximations of probable revenue to be generated from the projected mineral production. While the Tanbreez production is only postponed, I believe the value of this mining field remains intact with an IRR of 180%. While revenue in the past year is negligible, the key focus currently is on the commercial production of Tanbreez and Wolfberg, which continue to burn cash as previously demonstrated.

Recall that in my previous article, I noted that the company faces execution risk, which might affect its revenue performance. This is what we see: the earnings projection deteriorating due to high cash burn at this pre-revenue phase.

For companies at this stage, most valuation methods are irrelevant given the pre-revenue and cash-burning status. Therefore, I suggest investors keep in view the development status of the current projects, as any milestones may be a short-term catalyst of stock price. However, long-term speaking, nothing would matter more than the completion of the project.

Investment risks

I am reiterating this risk because after postponing projected initial production from FY2026 to 2028/2029, the company faces distressed financials, as previously shown. Well, I will use Altman’s Z-score to compare its position to its peers, but this does not mean the company faces a near-term bankruptcy risk; it only shows the level of financial distress. CRML’s 0.35 indicates severe financial distress, while SGML at 1.38 shows financial vulnerability, while MP at 3.73 shows somewhat financial stability.

This is why I believe CRML is currently in high anticipation rather than a verification following the initial production postponement to 2028/2029. It gives the impression that despite the efforts, such as joint ventures and partnerships discussed above, the company faces imminent financial distress. As such, this might lead to dilution when raising more capital to run the business.

That said, the score does not necessarily mean the company faces imminent financial risk, but it highlights the situation that the company is in.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my stance of taking a cautious outlook on CRML, mainly because of the delayed production timeline that has slipped from 2026 to late 2028/2029. At present, the company continues to experience a high cash burn rate without meaningful revenue. Therefore, I suggest investors keep an eye out while remaining cautious.

data.title: GLD: Explaining The Inverse Correlation Between Oil And Gold Prices
data.description:
Investment thesis.

Gold prices have been reacting in an arguably unpredictable way to the Iran war. One would ordinarily expect the safe-haven buying effect to drive the price of gold higher at a time of great upheaval. Nearly three weeks into this war, the price of gold is down just slightly, by about 5% as I write this. It continues to trade in inverse correlation to oil prices (CL1:COM), meaning that as oil prices continue to steadily rise, we might expect further downside.

The turning point for gold might not come for a few months. I expect that the Federal Reserve, as well as other central banks around the world, will do little to fight the coming wave of inflation, claiming it is transitory, which will be the trigger for gold prices to move higher again. With this in mind, I have the GLD ETF (GLD) as a buy, in expectation that gold prices will start a new, sustained rally once the market realizes that central banks will be in no mood to deal with rising inflation, triggered by higher oil prices.

About the GLD ETF.

The SPDR GLD ETF is designed to provide indirect exposure to the price of gold. The ETF covers outstanding shares with physical gold held in storage. It charges .4% for this service, which can add up over time.

Because there is a significant cumulative discrepancy in performance over a more prolonged period, I intend to eventually convert GLD shares into physical gold. At the moment, I find the convenience of trading the gold market instantly, versus the practical difficulties of trading physical gold, to be more than worth the convenience fee.

On the risk factor subject that is regularly debated when it comes to this ETF, I do not see the fact that one cannot redeem GLD shares for physical gold as a risk factor. I can sell the shares and buy physical gold at my convenience, as I already stated. On the often-brought-up issue of trust in the ETF always covering all of its shares with gold held in a vault, I do understand the concern, and I am personally not eager to over-expose my portfolio to this possibility. At the same time, after many years of worry on this matter, there is to date no evidence that it holds less than an adequate amount of gold to back its shares. It currently has 1,075.85 tonnes of gold in total holdings.

Understanding the recent inverse correlation between gold & oil.

Oil & gold prices have had a long history of trading in positive correlation. Some investors watch the gold-to-oil ratio as an indicator of whether gold or oil may be overpriced or perhaps underpriced. The fact that they are currently moving in the opposite direction may seem confusing and unexpected, especially given the added reason to expect gold prices to increase, namely, rising geopolitical tensions.

As we can see, since the start of the Iran war, GLD's share price declined slightly, even as oil prices advanced significantly. Before the start of hostilities, both oil & gold were experiencing a somewhat stagnant market, with the two assets moving more or less in tandem.

The main reason oil & gold are now trending in opposite directions is most likely that markets expect central banks to raise interest rates, which tends to be negative for gold prices. Bank of America (BAC) recently suggested that oil should be sold once it surpasses $100/barrel, because at that point, governments & central banks will take measures to rein in inflation.

Excessive debt & deficits make it less likely for central banks to raise rates.

The assumption that interest rates are set to rise, which is arguably the main factor driving gold prices lower, even as oil prices rise, may be deeply flawed. The main reason why central banks are unlikely to react the way that classical monetary theory suggests is that the fiscal health of many nations does not allow for it. An IMF map of the change in global government debt over the past two decades illustrates this problem rather well.

As we can see, while many of the World's major economies experienced elevated debt levels, most were manageable. Of the G7 members, only two had a debt-to-GDP ratio over 100% in 2005.

Currently, only one G7 nation, namely Germany, still has a government debt/GDP ratio under 100%, which will probably not last long, given its deteriorating fiscal & economic situation.

Notably, China, the World's second-largest economy, saw a severe deterioration in its fiscal situation over the past two decades, with its debt/GDP rising from 26% in 2025 to 102% currently. The US also saw a significant deterioration, with its debt/GDP ratio rising from 66% to 128% in the same period. Furthermore, most major economies are experiencing sizable fiscal pressures in the form of high deficits.

Based on the CBO's projections, America's debt/GDP ratio is already on a potentially dangerous trajectory.

As bad as the trajectory above may seem, higher interest rates would push the trajectory into a far steeper climb in the long-term in terms of debt/GDP.

As we can see, interest costs are increasingly the main driver of deficits. Higher interest rates would push deficits even higher. We should keep in mind that higher interest rates would also push the GDP growth trajectory lower, pushing the primary deficit higher as well, as tax revenues would decline and outlays would rise.

Allowing inflation to get out of control may seem risky, and it goes against the stated mandate of the Federal Reserve, but the risk of a structural fiscal crisis may outweigh inflationary risks. While it is an admittedly tough decision, I believe that the Federal Reserve as well as other central banks around the the World, including the ECB, will most likely opt to let inflation take its course, claiming that it is only transitory.

The post-war economy and gold price outlook.

As I stated in a recent article, if the Iran war lasts a total of 12 weeks, we are looking at a global demand destruction event spanning potentially the rest of the decade, needed to make up for a shortfall of about 1 billion barrels of oil, which will cause about 3% of global GDP to be lost. The mechanism that will make that happen will be the price of oil, which, in my view, will remain elevated for the rest of the decade relative to what we saw in the 2024-2025 period, when $60/barrel seemed like a new normal.

One of the key factors in the post-Iran war economy will be what I see as a largely stagnant global oil output outlook, contrary to the endless supply glut thesis perpetuated by the likes of the EIA & IEA in the past few years. As I have been pointing out since 2024, institutional guidance, estimates, and forecasts increasingly seem suspect, to say the least. The result is that on the other side of this war, a tighter-than-expected oil supply/demand balance awaits, even as governments and companies will look to replenish their depleted storage levels.

Between the aggressive emergency supply release of 400 million barrels that the IEA organized, coupled with the quiet release of emergency supplies by China, which is sitting on a sizable cushion that it built over the past few years, the immediate effect of the oil supply disruption in the Middle East is not immediately felt in most economies. I should note that it is not the case everywhere, since oil prices in India are currently trading about 40% higher than what we see in the US.

As long as the Iran war ends before the Summer, and Middle East oil exports resume at pre-war levels within a few months of the war's end, the US will probably avoid such an oil price spike scenario. However, it will also be faced with depleted commercial and government oil storage levels, same as China, Europe, Japan, India, and others. What this means is that global oil demand, including bids to secure oil for storage replenishment, will most likely be far above supply levels. In other words, the WTI price may not necessarily see a new record high through this crisis, but it may stay at a more elevated level for years to come.

Higher oil prices for a prolonged period mean heightened inflationary pressures working their way through the economy. At the same time, we will see lower economic growth, higher fiscal spending to compensate for lower growth and inflation, meaning higher deficits. It is a classic stagflationary trap, with central banks most likely operating in a state of near-paralysis as a result.

The key driver of gold prices heading higher within this context will most likely be central banks, with non-governmental investors playing the secondary role of effect amplifiers.

As we can see, the start of the Ukraine war and the resulting economic war between Russia & the Western Alliance triggered a central bank rush to buy gold. I believe that the current Iran war will trigger the next leg up in gold prices, driven by central banks looking to reduce their exposure to FX fiat currencies that are increasingly underpinned by economies that are declining in relative global economic and geopolitical relevance, and are entering a prolonged structural fiscal unsustainability phase.

Investment implications.

For the duration of the Iran war, gold prices are likely to continue to largely trade in inverse correlation to the price of oil. The price of oil will rise higher for the duration of this war, more slowly in the next few weeks, and then the rise will accelerate once we approach summer, and questions will arise about the willingness of governments to engage in additional drawdowns of their emergency oil storage volumes.

My overall gold & silver position in my portfolio is quite robust, sitting at about a fifth of my total portfolio. I am therefore not in a rush to buy the early dip we have seen since the start of the month of about 8% so far. I believe that there might be an additional 5% to 10% to go for the rest of the month if the war continues, at which point I intend to start buying additional GLD shares incrementally. I expect gold prices to start the second leg up once the war ends, or if it continues to the point where we are looking at a much steeper decline in the health of the global economy. The threshold of 12 weeks for this war to end, which is what I picked as a reference point, is probably where one can expect things to get a lot more severe if this war lasts longer than that, at which point, the current inverse correlation between oil & gold prices will end.

Risks to my thesis.

The obvious risk to my strategy of waiting for an additional decline in gold prices before I buy is that the Iran war could unexpectedly end, sending oil prices lower and gold prices higher, which means that I might miss out on adding to my position. Because my gold market outlook depends to a degree on assumptions about central bank buying, my assumption of gold prices rising in a post-war environment may prove to be flawed if central banks were to turn to net selling, for instance.

Conclusions.

The long-term fundamentals that led to a roughly 17-fold increase in gold prices since the turn of the century are still intact. The rising debt & deficits that we see from the same governments that issue the main FX reserve currencies, the slower global economic expansion, and the rising costs of commodities such as oil are all fundamental factors driving gold higher. None of those factors is likely to change in the coming years, which means that gold still has much upside going forward.

The Iran war may have a further negative impact on the metrics I cited. The short-term inverse correlation between oil prices and gold prices, caused by the Iran war, is, in my view, an emerging opportunity to buy more gold & gold-related assets. The GLD ETF offers a convenient investment vehicle, allowing investors to react swiftly to gold market conditions, which I see as an advantage in the current investment environment.

data.title: Endeavour Mining: African Gold At A Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Endeavour Mining (EDV:CA) (EDVMF) is the largest gold producer in West Africa with a total output of around 1.2 million ounces and a relatively low-cost structure (but operating expenses are increasing). At the current gold price, the company’s net sustaining margin on the mine operating level should be around $3,300/oz, resulting in close to $4B in net operating mine cash flow. This means Endeavour Mining has a good shot at reporting a record free cash flow result, even after deducting overhead expenses, net finance costs, and taxes. As the company is only active in West Africa, it is trading at a relatively low multiple compared to its peers that are focusing on Tier-1 jurisdictions.

I last covered the company almost 10 years ago, and although the total performance of +450% is quite good, the share price flatlined until the gold price really started to run, and the majority of that 450% total return was only generated in the past two years. Now that gold is trading at around $5000/oz, I wanted to have another look at this Africa-focused story.

A good production result equals strong cash flows in Q4

In the final quarter of 2025, Endeavour Mining produced just over 298,000 ounces of gold, and sold just under 302,000 ounces of gold at an average realized gold price of just under $3900/oz. The all-in sustaining cost was $1650/oz, indicating a very healthy margin of in excess of $2000/oz.

Looking at the full-year production, we see a total output of just over 1.2 million ounces of gold that were sold at an average price of just under $3250/oz. And with an all-in sustaining cost of $1433/oz, the net margin (pre-tax) on the mine level was approximately $1800/oz.

This resulted in a total revenue of $4.23B, an increase of approximately 50% compared to the preceding year, while the total mine operating earnings more than doubled to almost $2.1B.

The total reported operating income was $1.64B, which was more than four times the 2024 operating income, and after deducting all the finance expenses (including the loss on financial instruments, which is related to a $204M hedging loss), the pre-tax income was $1.34B, resulting in a net profit of $889M. And with a total of $780M in taxes and royalties paid to the governments of the countries it is active in, Endeavour Mining is playing an important role in the local economies by providing employment and paying taxes.

Of that $889M, $679M was attributable to the shareholders of Endeavour Mining, which worked out to an EPS of $2.80 per share.

Looking at the cash flow statement (shown below), the operating cash flow was approximately $1.66B, but this includes a $244M investment in the working capital while it also underestimates the taxes by approximately $90M. We should also deduct the $33M in lease payments and the $120M in dividends paid to non-controlling entities. This results in an adjusted operating cash flow of $1.66B.

We see the total capex was $530M, resulting in a net free cash flow of approximately $1.13B, of which approximately $1B was attributable to the common shareholders of Endeavour Mining. This cash flow was used to pay the dividend, buy back stock, and reduce the gross debt level by in excess of $500M, and this should have a positive impact on the net interest expenses going forward.

Endeavour Mining ended the year with $453M in cash and just under $600M in gross debt (excluding the sub-$100M in lease liabilities).

What can we expect in 2026?

The company’s guidance for 2026 is pretty straightforward, as the company has positioned itself as the largest gold producer in West Africa. For this year, Endeavour Mining expects a gold production between 1.09 million ounces of gold and 1.27 million ounces of gold at an all-in sustaining cost of $1600-1800/oz. The midpoint of the guidance indicates a production of just under 1.2 million ounces of gold at an AISC of $1700/oz, and it goes without saying that at a gold price of $5000 per ounce or even $4500/oz, the company will be printing cash.

On top of the $230M in sustaining capital expenditures (which is included in the AISC), Endeavour Mining expects to spend $270M in growth-related capex, which, of course, is easily covered by the anticipated free cash flow this year.

This also means the company’s minimum dividend is, of course, very well covered. Last year, Endeavour Mining established a $225M minimum dividend and paid $125M in supplementary dividends with an $85M share buyback, resulting in the company returning $435M to its shareholders.

For 2026, the minimum dividend has been established at $300M, and Endeavour’s plan is to increase this by $25M per year going forward. And of course, we can expect additional dividends and buybacks during excellent years.

The company mentions that at ‘prevailing gold prices,’ it plans to spend an additional $1B on shareholder rewards over a three-year period. If the gold price stays at $5000/oz (or even increases a bit, as the market is in contango), I think those additional dividends and shareholder rewards could exceed the $1B suggested by the company.

The risks associated with the investment thesis

I think the main risk is the geopolitical risk. Although the company appears to have good relationships with the authorities of the countries it is operating in, the situation obviously can change. But that's also why investors are able to pick up the stock at a discount to its peers. The image below shows how Endeavour Mining's free cash flow yield compares to some of the peers in its group.

Meanwhile, the company is trading at a clear discount versus the consensus estimates for the gold mining sector. As you can see below, Endeavour is trading at around 5 times EBITDA versus 9.5 times the sector average, while the P/CF ratio is just 5.8, which is just over half of the sector average.

So while, yes, there is an Africa risk, the company appears to be appropriately valued to take those risks into consideration. And with barely any net debt on its balance sheet (and likely showing a net cash position by the end of the current quarter), Endeavour Mining has access to plenty of liquidity to deal with any headwinds.

Investment thesis

Endeavour Mining has always traded at a discount due to its specific focus on West Africa, where a higher discount rate and lower earnings multiples are warranted. But that specific focus also helped Endeavour; rather than Africa being a division of a larger conglomerate that doesn’t always understand the local cultures, Endeavour appears to have built up more political capital and has been able to navigate the choppy waters a bit better.

I currently have no position in Endeavour Mining anymore, but I may have sold too soon, as the company remains attractive in the current gold price climate. At $4500 gold, I anticipate a net free cash flow of approximately US$2B or US$8/share. That makes the current share price still reasonable.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: On Holding: Still A Long Way To Run, But Waiting For A Stumble To Buy
data.description:
On Holding (ONON) delivered a great earnings report on March 3, with FY 2025 revenue surpassing CHF 3 billion for the first time and margins reaching record levels. The company is performing ahead of the targets outlined in the 2023 Investor Day; however, the stock fell significantly following the release. The market seems disappointed by a decline in GAAP net income and FY 2026 revenue guidance that came in below analysts' estimates.

In our view, there is nothing broken structurally, as FX short-term fluctuations are not related to business performance, a deceleration in growth is expected as a company scales, the guidance was deliberately conservative by management, and ON has plenty of runway to continue growing. We rate ON as a Hold with a $42 price target.

Because of the quality of the business, the management, and the opportunity for profitable growth, we feel tempted to assign ON a Buy rating, but the current stock price does not provide enough margin of safety. This is essential in our investment process, and our sensitivity analysis suggests that in the low $30s, the risk/reward profile becomes asymmetrically favorable. Consequently, we decide to remain on the sidelines awaiting a more attractive entry point.

Strategic Scaling

On Holding was established in Zurich in 2010 by former triathlete Olivier Bernhard, David Allemann, and Caspar Coppetti with the aim of creating a shoe that felt like running on clouds. That idea, which demonstrates that differentiation based on product performance is in ON's DNA, was the foundation of the CloudTec patented technology. This consists of a series of hollows in the sole that compress on impact and lock on take-off, which became the visual signature of the brand. Now, ON sells its products in more than 90 countries, through DTC and selected wholesale channels.

Apart from its focus on differentiation, we believe management is strategically scaling the brand, protecting brand equity and profitability through a combination of pricing discipline, channel management, and relentless focus on innovation.

On Holding boasts industry-leading margins that resemble those of the luxury sector. On's 62.8% gross margin stands well above established peers like Nike (NKE) and Adidas (ADDYY), by roughly 20 and 10 percentage points, respectively. This outperformance is a result of the company's pricing discipline. ON avoids aggressive discounting and outlet stores, instead managing excess inventory through its Onward circular commerce platform. The Swiss brand managed to maintain full price execution during the highly promotional holiday season.

Additional evidence that the company is not chasing growth at all costs was the decision to close non-strategic wholesale partner stores in Europe to protect the brand. Based on this, we can conclude that the management team is willing to take short-term pain for future gains, which we see as a highly positive sign for long-term value creation.

Another factor that we like about this growth story is the company's relentless focus on innovation, not only in product or design but also in manufacturing technology and cost control.

As an example, Lightspray is a cutting-edge seamless sneaker upper that is produced through filament spraying. In Q4 2025, the firm announced a new plant in Busan, South Korea, with 30x capacity improvement compared to the previous system. Producing the upper requires 200 assembly steps in multiple facilities and is also labor-intensive. In the Busan plant, the process will be reduced to a single three-minute step, performed by a robot, with minimal material waste.

Fundamental Strength vs. Market Noise

On Holding reported solid Q4 and FY 2025 earnings on March 3. Net revenue surpassed CHF 3 billion for the first time with a 35.6% growth rate on a constant currency basis. Gross margin reached a new high at 62.8%, and adjusted EBITDA came in at CHF 567 million, which is a 46% increase from a year ago. Finally, the company is performing ahead of the targets outlined in the 2023 Investor Day.

The performance was strong, but the market did not digest it well, as the stock declined 6% the following day, and it is now 15% down from its pre-earnings level. There are two factors that caused concern among investors: first, the 15.9% decline in GAAP net income, and second, a 2026 guidance below analysts' consensus.

Regarding the decline in net income, this was due to an unrealized FX loss of CHF 173 million caused by a stronger Swiss franc. On Holding generates income in multiple currencies but uses the Swiss franc as its reporting currency. When the franc appreciates, it triggers a non-operating accounting charge, which says nothing about the underlying quality of the business. Also, if the company had chosen to report in US dollars, as other major sportswear companies do, it wouldn't have recorded an FX loss of such magnitude. As mentioned before, the metrics that truly matter, such as constant currency growth and profitability margins, were record-breaking. We believe a long-term assessment of a business of ON's quality should not put much emphasis on short-term currency fluctuations.

On the guidance front, the "at least" 23% CC growth rate projected for 2026 fell short of analyst expectations, as they were expecting it to be closer to historical levels. In our view, there are three factors that investors should bear in mind before rapidly concluding that the growth story is near the end.

First, it is simple arithmetic. The firm was able to generate CHF 3 billion in 2025, so growing 30% on that base would mean that the company is adding CHF 900 million in a single year, which is more than ON's total revenue of FY 2021. The math of compounding makes high-growth rates increasingly difficult to maintain as the base expands, so this is not necessarily a sign of trouble; it is just how scaling works.

Second, the "at least" 23% growth rate is not a midpoint estimate; it is a floor. In addition, management's philosophy regarding guidance is under-promise and over-deliver. As can be seen in the table below, this statement is supported by management's track record. When comparing the guidance issued in Q4 each year vs. what ON actually delivers, one can see that the Swiss brand beats every metric by a wide margin. For example, last year they forecasted 27%, while the actual print came in at 35.6%, an 860 bps beat. If we go back to FY2023, we'll see the gap is even wider. Also, the pattern is the exact same with margins, so this is not circumstantial outperformance; it reflects how conservative the leadership team is at managing expectations.

Third, it is worth noting that ON could easily chase faster growth at all costs, but they decided not to do that. By engaging in discounting, flooding outlet stores, or pushing deep into lower-quality wholesale accounts, they could probably reach the 30% mark in 2026. However, management has deliberately chosen not to do that, protecting brand equity and profitability. While this could have an impact on short-term stock performance, we see this as highly beneficial for the long-term.

And at the same time, yes, we could grow faster, but we put a lot of focus on growing in a premium way, to grow in a highly qualitative way, and to be very selective on the wholesale partners that we work with … to be very selective on the new retail locations that we are opening in. And for us, it's all about the long term and just being able to build a brand that can maintain very strong growth for a long period of time.

Source: Martin Hoffmann, CEO - Post-Q4 2025 interview

The Growth Runway

Beyond the 2026 guidance, the question is not whether top-line growth will decelerate because it will, but how long ON can grow above industry rates. We think the Q4 earnings presentation provided some data points that signal that growth is not running out of steam and it can last several years.

Brand awareness rose 10% and is now at 30%, while legacy rivals Adidas and Nike both exceed the 85% level. Each percentage point of additional awareness means new customers with no prior brand experience, so ON has a large untapped market.

Also, APAC grew 106.7% CC YoY, exceeding CHF 500 million in annual revenue for the first time, and two Hong Kong stores delivered the highest productivity globally during the holiday period. The company is planning 10 to 15 new store openings this year. On top of that, same-store productivity increased ~20% YoY, indicating that unit economics are improving across the DTC channel and the growth prospects are not limited to adding new doors.

In terms of product category, net sales of Apparel and Accessories combined surged 84.4% CC YoY and now account for 7% of revenue. This represents significant progress towards the goal of evolving into a head-to-toe sportswear brand. Also, management noted that apparel is increasingly becoming a driver of customer acquisition, with 10% of new customers entering the brand through this category.

Based on all this information, we believe the runway is considerably longer than the post-earnings selloff implies.

Valuation

Before estimating our intrinsic value for the stock, it is worth examining how the market is pricing ON relative to its peers. The table below shows that On Holding trades at a premium relative to most of its comparables, but that may be due to its higher growth rate and superior gross margin, even if its EBITDA margin remains below that of some of its competitors. Deckers (DECK) is the closest comparable and trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 9.57x, but it is guiding for lower growth and gross margin than On Holding. On the other hand, lululemon's (LULU) multiple is at 7.14x with a higher EBITDA margin, but ON is growing faster.

While On Holding is trading at a premium to its peers, and there may be reasons supporting that, our DCF model suggests that the stock price is slightly below its intrinsic value.

As a base case, we assume a 10-year growth runway, so the revenue growth rate is set at 25% for FY2026 and gradually converges to a terminal rate of 3.5% in 2035, corresponding to 2.5% inflation plus 1% real growth. EBITDA margin gradually expands from 19% to 24.4%, which is below DECK and LULU levels. Terminal value is determined through a GGM, and the 9% WACC reflects a blend of the current all-equity cost and a capital structure that is expected to evolve into a modest use of debt, with its associated interest tax shield, as the business matures. All other assumptions and the valuation outcome are shown below.

As the table illustrates, our base case yields a $42 intrinsic value for the stock (~6% upside), but this figure is highly sensitive to the terminal value, which represents 72% of total enterprise value. This is not unusual for a high-growth company like ON, but given the wide range of possible outcomes, it is beneficial to model the terminal stage under different scenarios to get a better perspective. In our sensitivity analysis, the terminal growth rate ranges from 2.5% to 4.5%, and the cost of capital ranges from 8% to 10%. Consequently, the sensitivity analysis yields a range between $32 and $65. At a current stock price of $39.79, the outcomes are roughly balanced around the base case. Based on this, we rate On as a Hold.

Risks

The last earnings release showed that ON's investors are subject to sentiment swings, even with clean operational performance. At current valuation levels, a downward guidance revision or any signal that management cannot meet its growth projections can lead to multiple compression. Additionally, a stronger CHF could introduce translation noise, but we believe investors should monitor CC growth rates and look through short-term FX volatility.

Fashion risk is always a threat for companies in this industry. Brand relevance can rapidly erode if product offering is not perceived as differentiated, innovation stalls, or the brand fails to resonate with consumer trends. That said, in our view, the sportswear market is undergoing a clear fragmentation, with smaller, high-quality, performance-oriented brands taking share from legacy players. On Holding emerges as a beneficiary of this trend, and we are not seeing any sign of reversion in this dynamic.

China is a key growth driver within the APAC geography, and while sales data remains strong, ON is still a relatively new brand in that market. The firm is competing with local brands there, and consumer nationalism could influence consumer preferences.

Regarding tariffs, approximately 90% of footwear and 65% of apparel and accessories are sourced from Vietnam. While management is partially mitigating the impact of tariffs, a more protectionist business environment would weigh on the company's margins.

The Bottom Line

On Holding is probably one of the most compelling brand scaling stories in the market. It is a great business led by top-tier management with a long-term focus, the discipline to grow the company profitably and protect brand equity, and an under-promise and over-deliver mentality. A post-earnings selloff triggered by FX losses and, more importantly, lower-than-expected guidance has pushed the stock slightly below our intrinsic value estimate. We like the business, the management running it, and we think the brand has plenty of runway to grow; however, our assessment suggests there is still not enough margin of safety to initiate a long position. For these reasons, we rate ON as a Hold and remain on the sidelines awaiting a more attractive entry point in the low $30s.

data.title: Micron: HBM Leads To Profit Explosion
data.description:
Semiconductor and high bandwidth memory (“HBM”) specialist Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), published better-than-expected earnings and revenue for the second fiscal quarter, which were boosted by unprecedented demand for the company’s memory and storage products. The Data Center market, which is in hot pursuit of graphics processing units as well as high bandwidth memory, is booming and has led Micron to report a near-tripling of its revenues in the second fiscal quarter. The demand backdrop for both DRAM and NAND is very strong, which underpins a robust outlook for Micron for the third fiscal quarter as well. I like Micron here as a secular chip play, especially given the massive profit explosion in its core business, and believe investors should buy the drop following the Q2 '26 earnings report.

Previous rating

I rated shares of Micron a Strong Buy—A Lever On AI Growth—in my last coverage in December. Since then, shares have skyrocketed 86%, and they still have more upside potential, in my opinion. Because of the continual expansion of the total addressable market for high-bandwidth memory products, the firm's earnings and margin situation have greatly improved lately. In the second fiscal quarter, Micron generated significant top-line growth and achieved a material growth acceleration vs. the previous quarter. Micron is a major beneficiary of the growth explosion in the Data Center industry, and with strong industry fundamentals prevailing, I continue to see the chip play as a strong buy.

Soaring HBM demand leads to a profit explosion

Micron reported better-than-expected results on the bottom and the top lines for its second fiscal quarter on Wednesday: the semiconductor company reported non-GAAP earnings of $12.20 per share, beating the consensus by a solid $3.23 per share. Revenues, due to an AI-driven growth acceleration in the Data Center segment, came in at $23.9B, beating the average prediction by $4.4B.

Micron was laser-focused on ramping up its HBM product shipments in the second fiscal quarter, which are needed in Data Centers to run large language models. Together with GPUs, fast-processing HBM is key to running compute-intensive AI applications, which obviously makes them a hot commodity right now.

In the most recent quarter, Micron benefited from surging demand for HBM and published revenues of $23.9B, showing 196% year-over-year growth. Compared to the previous quarter, Micron's top-line growth accelerated a massive 139 PP on a Y/Y basis. As a result, the core Data Center business saw record revenues of $5.7B and generated 211% year-over-year growth, highlighting a strong demand picture from this specific industry. As I indicated in my last work on Micron, the chip company has sold out its entire HBM capacity for the current calendar year.

Most importantly, on the back of soaring demand for high-bandwidth memory products, which are very high margin (70%+), Micron posted record profitability. The growth rates on a number of metrics were beyond impressive: for instance, Micron’s non-GAAP gross margin soared to 75% in the second quarter (+18 PP Q/Q), while its free cash flows increased to a quarterly record of $6.9B, showing 705% year-over-year growth.

$M Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 Q2'26 Y/Y Growth
Revenue $8,053 $9,301 $11,315 $13,643 $23,860 196%
Non-GAAP Gross Margin $3,053 $3,623 $5,169 $7,753 $17,876 486%
Non-GAAP Gross Margin (%) 38% 39% 46% 57% 75% 98%
Free Cash Flow $857 $1,949 $803 $3,906 $6,899 705%
Free Cash Flow Margin 11% 21% 7% 29% 29% 172%

Source: Author.

Potential for margin gains on strong HBM4 demand

As for the outlook for Q3 ’26, Micron guided for $33.5B in revenues, highlighting 40% quarter-over-quarter growth. Most importantly, the guidance implies a significant gross margin expansion to 81% (implied 6 PP Q/Q up-lift) as more revenues are expected to shift to the high-margin HBM business throughout the quarter. Micron expects DRAM bit shipments to grow in the low-20s percentage range in the third fiscal quarter, and the semiconductor company expects the HBM market to remain in a supply deficit for the rest of the year, all of which bodes well for margin growth.

The strong outlook is backed by Micron ramping up HBM4 shipments related to NVIDIA Corporation's (NVDA) launch of the Vera Rubin GPU platform this year. Soaring sales for Nvidia's newest AI GPU therefore have the potential to lead to windfall profits for Micron in the next 12-24 months. I have previously argued that the availability of a new GPU (post-Blackwell) could trigger an entirely new chip upgrade cycle in the Data Center industry, which would obviously be highly beneficial to Micron's earnings growth and margin prospects, especially in its core Data Center segment.

Micron’s valuation is still cheap

Micron’s shares are cheap based on earnings, which is due to the fact that the chip company has seen past cycles of massive CapEx ramps and surging demand for its memory and storage products. During such periods, Micron's profits tend to surge, which compresses the P/E ratio. However, as I have said elsewhere repeatedly, Data Center-driven CapEx spending appears to be secular in nature.

Major hyperscalers like Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) are massively increasing their CapEx spending levels to roll out their AI products and services at scale. Amazon, for instance, is set to increase its spending level to $200B just this year.

Shares of Micron are currently trading below the longer-term, 3-year average P/E ratio, which is due to the semiconductor enterprise seeing a massive wave of EPS estimate upside revisions this year. Micron is expected to grow its earnings by 59% annually going forward, according to Seeking Alpha, highlighting an attractive growth setup for investors, especially if Nvidia's Vera Rubin GPUs are as strong in demand as Blackwell GPUs.

Micron is currently priced at a P/E ratio, based on next year's earnings, of 8.1X, which is about 42% below the historical, 3-year P/E ratio. I believe that shares have the potential to revalue to the longer-term mean here, 13.8X, in large part because I see AI-driven CapEx spending (the main catalyst for Micron's HBM ramp) as a secular phenomenon with many more years of growth runway. A 13.8X forward P/E ratio implies a fair value for shares of Micron of $736 per share, indicating 71% upside potential.

Risks with Micron

The biggest risk for Micron, given its increasing dependence on HBM shipments, is a contraction in the Data Center market and a potential rollback of AI expansion plans in the industry. A rollback of CapEx programs and AI Data Center buildout plans would likely hurt demand for high-bandwidth memory, which is Micron’s fastest-growing and most lucrative business. Poor GPU adoption of Nvidia's Vera Rubin chip would also be a potential cause for concern. Currently, I see the margin growth trend as fully intact and would only reverse my strong buy rating if the semiconductor platform were to see a drop of its gross margins below 70% going forward.

Final thoughts

Micron submitted a strong earnings report card for the second fiscal quarter on Thursday that saw multiple records in terms of revenues, non-GAAP operating income, and free cash flow. The outlook for the third fiscal quarter is very strong as well, with the company expecting a supply deficit in both DRAM and NAND markets to carry on throughout the year.

Shares of Micron dropped ~7% after the earnings report, likely due to investor fears about margins topping out, but the business setup is very strong right now: the firm's guidance for Q3 ’26 strongly implies that margins will continue to grow amid red-hot demand for the company’s high-bandwidth memory products that are getting stacked in Data Centers across the world. I like the MU dip here and would recommend investors buy aggressively.

data.title: Powell's Pause: A Gamble Wrapped In Uncertainty
data.description:
The Federal Reserve has a deep pool of resources for analyzing the economy to support its mission to adjust monetary policy to match current and expected macro conditions. But sometimes a central bank’s vaunted research machine offers insights no sharper than whatever you’d get from chatting with a guy waiting at the bus stop. The present moment is one of those times, thanks to the uncertainty surrounding the ongoing war in Iraq.

Federal Reserve Chairman Powell admitted as much yesterday after the Fed announced that it left its key interest rate unchanged at a 4.50-4.75% range.

“The thing I really want to emphasize is, nobody knows,” Powell said, citing the Iran war as the main source of indecision and uncertainty. “The economic effects could be bigger, they could be smaller, they could be much smaller, they could be much bigger. We just don’t know.”

No one does. One man in the White House holds the key for the war’s path, at least in terms of the US role. But with so many moving parts to the conflict and an endless array of rapidly evolving economic and financial implications, the distribution of outcomes could hardly be much wider or more shrouded in a fog of unknowing.

The main concerns from a macro perspective: the war could raise inflation, slow growth, or some degree of both – the dreaded stagflation scenario. The arrival of those twin challenges would be uniquely difficult for a central bank because the monetary toolbox tends to only work effectively on one problem or the other. That raises the difficult calculus that any decision could push one problem in the right direction while worsening the other. Tightening policy cools inflation but deepens unemployment, while easing policy supports hiring but fuels inflation.

The business of central banking goes on, of course, even if the best course of action in the current climate is to do nothing and wait to see what happens.

One point of clarity for the market at the moment is that interest rate cuts are now considered unlikely any time soon.
